subject: mathematics
ncdc_competency: The learner applies integration techniques to find areas, volumes, and mean values, and solves problems involving indefinite and definite integrals in real-life and mathematical contexts.

lessons.title: Indefinite Integration — The Reverse of Differentiation

content.body: Integration is the second fundamental operation of calculus — the reverse of differentiation. If differentiation asks 'given a function, find its rate of change', integration asks 'given a rate of change, find the original function'. This reversal process is called antidifferentiation or indefinite integration. Integration is one of the most powerful tools in all of mathematics: it computes areas, volumes, lengths, accumulated totals, work done by forces, electric charge, and probability distributions. Every physical quantity that is found by accumulation — total rainfall, total displacement, total energy — involves integration. In Uganda, integration is applied in hydrology (computing total river flow), engineering (calculating structural loads), and economics (finding consumer surplus and total revenue).
The Indefinite Integral — Definition and Notation

If F'(x) = f(x), then F(x) is called an antiderivative of f(x). Because the derivative of any constant is zero, if F(x) is one antiderivative, then F(x)+C is also an antiderivative for any constant C. The indefinite integral captures all antiderivatives at once.

Indefinite integral:
∫ f(x) dx = F(x) + C

where F'(x) = f(x) and C is the constant of integration.

The symbol ∫ is an elongated S (for 'sum'). The dx indicates we are integrating with respect to x.

Verification: always check by differentiating — d/dx[F(x)+C] must equal f(x).

Power rule for integration (reverse of power rule for differentiation):
∫ xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C     provided n ≠ −1

Key standard integrals:
∫ xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C    (n ≠ −1)
∫ k dx = kx + C               (constant)
∫ x⁻¹ dx = ln|x| + C         (special case n=−1)
∫ eˣ dx = eˣ + C

Linearity rules:
∫ [f(x)+g(x)] dx = ∫f(x)dx + ∫g(x)dx
∫ kf(x) dx = k∫f(x)dx

content.title: Example 2: Integrals requiring simplification first
content.body:
Always simplify before integrating — expand brackets or split fractions.

a) ∫ x(x+3)² dx
Expand: x(x²+6x+9) = x³+6x²+9x
∫(x³+6x²+9x) dx = x⁴/4 + 2x³ + 9x²/2 + C

b) ∫ (x²+2)/x dx = ∫(x + 2/x) dx = x²/2 + 2ln|x| + C

c) ∫ (√x + 1/√x)² dx
Expand: (√x)² + 2(√x)(1/√x) + (1/√x)² = x + 2 + x⁻¹
∫(x + 2 + x⁻¹) dx = x²/2 + 2x + ln|x| + C
Finding the Constant of Integration
When additional information is given — such as a point the curve passes through — we can find the specific value of C and determine the particular antiderivative.

content.title: Example 3: Finding C from initial conditions
content.body:
A curve has dy/dx = 3x² − 4x + 1 and passes through the point (2, 5). Find y.

Integrate: y = ∫(3x²−4x+1) dx = x³ − 2x² + x + C

Substitute (2, 5): 5 = 8 − 8 + 2 + C → 5 = 2 + C → C = 3

Answer: y = x³ − 2x² + x + 3

Verification: at x=2: y = 8−8+2+3 = 5 ✓
And dy/dx = 3x²−4x+1 ✓

Motion and integration: If the velocity v of a vehicle is known as a function of time t, then integrating gives the displacement: s = ∫v dt. If acceleration a is known, integrating gives velocity: v = ∫a dt. A traffic engineer monitoring vehicles on Entebbe Road can use integration to compute total distance travelled from a velocity-time record, or to find the velocity at any time from an acceleration profile. Integration transforms instantaneous rates into cumulative totals.

questions.question: Find ∫ (6x² − 4x + 3) dx
Evaluate ∫ x^(−3) dx
questions.explanation: ∫x⁻³dx = x⁻²/(−2)+C = −x⁻²/2+C = −1/(2x²)+C. Options A and C are equivalent.

questions.question: Find ∫ (x+1)² dx by expanding first
Integrate ∫ (3x² + 2)/x² dx
questions.explanation: Split: ∫(3+2x⁻²)dx=3x+2x⁻¹/(−1)+C=3x−2x⁻¹+C=3x−2/x+C. A and B are the same.

questions.question: A curve passes through (1, 4) and dy/dx = 4x − 1. Find y.
Find ∫ √(x³) dx
Which of these is NOT a valid antiderivative of f(x) = 2x?
questions.explanation: d/dx(2x²)=4x ≠ 2x. So 2x² is NOT an antiderivative of 2x. All forms x²+C (for any constant C) are valid antiderivatives since d/dx(x²+C)=2x.

questions.question: ∫ (x² − 1)(x + 1) dx simplifies and integrates to:

lessons.title: Integration by Substitution and Integrals of Trig Functions

content.body: The power rule handles polynomials, but many important functions — composite functions, trigonometric functions, and exponentials — require more powerful techniques. Integration by substitution is the analogue of the chain rule in differentiation: it transforms a difficult integral into a simpler one by changing the variable. Alongside this, integrals of the standard trigonometric functions are essential building blocks for physics, engineering, and advanced mathematics.
Integration by Substitution

Integration by substitution (reverse chain rule):

If the integrand contains a composite function f(g(x))×g'(x), let u=g(x):

∫ f(g(x)) × g'(x) dx = ∫ f(u) du

Procedure:
1. Choose u = (inner function or a key part of the integrand)
2. Differentiate: du/dx = g'(x), so du = g'(x)dx
3. Substitute: replace g(x) with u and g'(x)dx with du
4. Integrate with respect to u
5. Substitute back: replace u with g(x)

Pattern recognition — look for:
• ∫ f(ax+b) dx → u = ax+b
• ∫ f(xⁿ) × xⁿ⁻¹ dx → u = xⁿ
• ∫ f'(x)/f(x) dx = ln|f(x)| + C  (logarithm form)
• ∫ f(x)f'(x) dx = [f(x)]²/2 + C  (power form)

content.title: Example 1: Substitution with linear inner function
Example 2: Substitution with composite functions
content.body:
a) ∫ 2x(x²+3)⁴ dx
Let u=x²+3. du=2x dx. (The 2x dx matches perfectly!)
∫ u⁴ du = u⁵/5 + C = (x²+3)⁵/5 + C

b) ∫ x²√(x³+1) dx
Let u=x³+1. du=3x²dx → x²dx=du/3.
∫ √u × (du/3) = (1/3) × (2/3)u^(3/2)+C = 2u^(3/2)/9+C
= 2(x³+1)^(3/2)/9 + C

c) ∫ sin(x)cos(x) dx
Let u=sinx. du=cosxdx.
∫ u du = u²/2+C = sin²x/2 + C

Alternative: let u=cosx. du=−sinxdx.
∫ −u du = −u²/2+C = −cos²x/2+C
Both are valid — they differ by a constant since sin²x/2+cos²x/2=1/2.
Integrals of Trigonometric Functions

Standard trigonometric integrals:

∫ sin x dx = −cos x + C
∫ cos x dx = sin x + C
∫ sec²x dx = tan x + C
∫ cosec²x dx = −cot x + C
∫ sec x tan x dx = sec x + C
∫ cosec x cot x dx = −cosec x + C

With chain rule / substitution:
∫ sin(ax+b) dx = −cos(ax+b)/a + C
∫ cos(ax+b) dx = sin(ax+b)/a + C
∫ sec²(ax+b) dx = tan(ax+b)/a + C

Using double angle identities for sin²x and cos²x:
∫ sin²x dx = ∫ (1−cos2x)/2 dx = x/2 − sin2x/4 + C
∫ cos²x dx = ∫ (1+cos2x)/2 dx = x/2 + sin2x/4 + C

content.title: Example 3: Integrating trigonometric functions
content.body:
a) ∫ sin(3x) dx = −cos(3x)/3 + C

b) ∫ cos(x/2) dx = sin(x/2)/(1/2) + C = 2sin(x/2) + C

c) ∫ (2cos x − 3sin x) dx = 2sinx + 3cosx + C

d) ∫ sin²x dx = ∫(1−cos2x)/2 dx
= (1/2)∫(1−cos2x)dx
= x/2 − sin(2x)/4 + C

e) ∫ cos²(3x) dx = ∫(1+cos6x)/2 dx
= x/2 + sin(6x)/12 + C

f) ∫ sin x cos x dx = (1/2)∫sin2x dx = −cos2x/4 + C
(Using sin2x=2sinxcosx, so sinxcosx=sin2x/2)

Electrical engineering application: Alternating current in Uganda's national grid follows I = I₀sin(ωt). The energy dissipated in a resistor R over one period T is proportional to ∫₀ᵀ I²dt = I₀²∫₀ᵀ sin²(ωt)dt. Using the identity sin²(ωt) = (1−cos2ωt)/2, this integral gives the RMS (root mean square) current — the effective DC equivalent. Uganda Electricity Transmission Company engineers use this calculation to size transformers and transmission lines correctly for the national grid.

questions.question: Find ∫ (5x+2)⁷ dx by substitution
Evaluate ∫ 3x²(x³+1)⁵ dx using substitution
Find ∫ cos(4x) dx
Find ∫ sin²(x) dx using the double angle identity
Find ∫ x/√(x²+4) dx
Find ∫ (2x+1)/(x²+x+3) dx
questions.explanation: The numerator (2x+1) is the derivative of the denominator (x²+x+3). This matches ∫f'(x)/f(x)dx=ln|f(x)|+C. Answer: ln|x²+x+3|+C.

questions.question: Evaluate ∫ sec²(3x+1) dx
Find ∫ cos²(x) dx using the identity cos²x = (1+cos2x)/2

lessons.title: Definite Integrals — Evaluation and the Fundamental Theorem

content.body: The indefinite integral gives a family of functions; the definite integral gives a specific NUMBER — the area under a curve between two limits. This profound connection between the area problem and antidifferentiation is the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, one of the most important results in all of mathematics. It was discovered independently by Newton and Leibniz in the 17th century and transformed science and engineering forever.
The Definite Integral and the Fundamental Theorem

The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus:

If F'(x) = f(x), then:

∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx = [F(x)]ₐᵇ = F(b) − F(a)

Where:
• a is the lower limit, b is the upper limit
• F(x) is any antiderivative of f(x)
• The constant of integration C cancels: [F(x)+C]ₐᵇ = F(b)+C−F(a)−C = F(b)−F(a)

Notation: [F(x)]ₐᵇ means 'evaluate F(x) at x=b then subtract F(x) at x=a'

Properties of definite integrals:
• ∫ₐᵃ f(x)dx = 0  (zero width → zero area)
• ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = −∫ᵦₐ f(x)dx  (swap limits → change sign)
• ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = ∫ₐᶜ f(x)dx + ∫ᶜᵇ f(x)dx  (split at interior point c)
• ∫ₐᵇ kf(x)dx = k∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx
• ∫ₐᵇ [f(x)±g(x)]dx = ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx ± ∫ₐᵇ g(x)dx

content.title: Example 2: Definite integrals with substitution
content.body:
Method 1 — Change limits with substitution:
∫₀¹ 2x(x²+1)³ dx

Let u=x²+1. du=2xdx.
When x=0: u=1. When x=1: u=2. (Change the limits!)

∫₁² u³ du = [u⁴/4]₁² = 16/4 − 1/4 = 15/4

Method 2 — Substitute back, then evaluate:
Indefinite: ∫2x(x²+1)³dx = (x²+1)⁴/4 + C
Definite: [(x²+1)⁴/4]₀¹ = (1+1)⁴/4 − (0+1)⁴/4 = 16/4 − 1/4 = 15/4 ✓
Even and Odd Functions — Symmetry Shortcuts

For integration over symmetric intervals [−a, a]:

If f(x) is an EVEN function (f(−x)=f(x), symmetric about y-axis):
∫₋ₐᵃ f(x)dx = 2∫₀ᵃ f(x)dx

If f(x) is an ODD function (f(−x)=−f(x), rotational symmetry about origin):
∫₋ₐᵃ f(x)dx = 0

Examples:
∫₋₂² x⁴ dx = 2∫₀² x⁴ dx = 2[x⁵/5]₀² = 2(32/5) = 64/5  (x⁴ is even)
∫₋π^π sin x dx = 0  (sin x is odd)
∫₋₁¹ (x³+x) dx = 0  (x³+x is odd since (−x)³+(−x) = −(x³+x))

Checking results: Always sanity-check definite integrals. A definite integral of a non-negative function over [a,b] must be ≥ 0 (it represents area). If you get a negative result when the function is above the x-axis between a and b, you have made an error. Also verify by estimating: if the function averages roughly h over width (b−a), the integral should be approximately h(b−a).

questions.question: Evaluate ∫₀² (3x² + 2x) dx
Find ∫₁⁴ 1/√x dx
Evaluate ∫₀^(π/4) cos(2x) dx
If ∫₂⁵ f(x)dx = 7 and ∫₂⁸ f(x)dx = 11, find ∫₅⁸ f(x)dx
Evaluate ∫₋₂² x³ dx using the odd function property
questions.explanation: x³ is an odd function (f(−x)=−f(x)). For any odd function: ∫₋ₐᵃ f(x)dx=0.

questions.question: Use substitution to evaluate ∫₀¹ x(1+x²)³ dx (change limits)
∫₀^π sin²x dx equals:
Evaluate ∫₁² (x³−x⁻²) dx

lessons.title: Area Under a Curve and Area Between Curves

content.body: The most direct geometric application of the definite integral is computing areas. The area under a curve and above the x-axis is given by a definite integral — this is precisely what the Fundamental Theorem connects to antidifferentiation. However, when parts of a curve lie below the x-axis, or when we want the area between two curves, we must be careful with signs and strategy. These area calculations are used throughout science and engineering: computing the work done by a variable force, the impulse of a force over time, the amount of material needed to fill a space, and even economic measures like consumer surplus.
Area Between a Curve and the x-axis

For a continuous curve y=f(x):

Area ABOVE x-axis between x=a and x=b (where f(x)≥0):
A = ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx

When the curve goes BELOW the x-axis (f(x)<0 for some region):
The integral gives a NEGATIVE value for the parts below the x-axis.

To find the TOTAL (positive) area:
1. Find all roots of f(x)=0 between a and b (x-intercepts)
2. Split the integral at each root
3. Take the ABSOLUTE VALUE of each part
4. Add the absolute values

Total area = |∫ₐᶜ f(x)dx| + |∫ᶜᵇ f(x)dx|

CRITICAL DISTINCTION:
• SIGNED area = ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx  (can be negative)
• ACTUAL area = sum of |each section|  (always positive)

content.title: Example 1: Area under a curve above the x-axis
content.body:
Find the area enclosed by y = x² − 4x + 3, the x-axis, and the lines x=0 and x=4.

Step 1 — Find roots: x²−4x+3=0 → (x−1)(x−3)=0 → x=1 and x=3.

The curve is above x-axis for x<1 and x>3; below x-axis for 1<x<3.

Step 2 — Split the integral:
A = |∫₀¹(x²−4x+3)dx| + |∫₁³(x²−4x+3)dx| + |∫₃⁴(x²−4x+3)dx|

Integrate: F(x) = x³/3 − 2x² + 3x

F(0)=0, F(1)=1/3−2+3=4/3, F(3)=9−18+9=0, F(4)=64/3−32+12=64/3−20=4/3

A = |4/3−0| + |0−4/3| + |4/3−0|
= 4/3 + 4/3 + 4/3 = 4 square units

Note: ∫₀⁴(x²−4x+3)dx = F(4)−F(0) = 4/3−0 = 4/3 ≠ 4. The signed integral gives 4/3 but total area is 4!
Area Between Two Curves

Area between curves y=f(x) (upper) and y=g(x) (lower) from x=a to x=b:

A = ∫ₐᵇ [f(x) − g(x)] dx

Where f(x) ≥ g(x) throughout [a, b] (f is the upper curve).

If the curves cross within [a, b]:
1. Find all intersection points
2. Split the region at each crossing
3. In each sub-region, determine which curve is on top
4. Add up the absolute values of sub-region areas

To find intersection points: solve f(x)=g(x) → these give the x-limits.

content.title: Example 2: Area between two curves
content.body:
Find the area enclosed between y = x² and y = x+2.

Step 1 — Find intersections:
x² = x+2 → x²−x−2=0 → (x−2)(x+1)=0 → x=−1 and x=2.

Step 2 — Determine upper/lower curve between x=−1 and x=2:
At x=0: y=x²=0 and y=x+2=2. So y=x+2 is on top.

Step 3 — Integrate:
A = ∫₋₁² [(x+2) − x²] dx
= ∫₋₁² (x+2−x²) dx
= [x²/2 + 2x − x³/3]₋₁²
= (4/2+4−8/3) − (1/2−2+1/3)
= (2+4−8/3) − (−3/2+1/3)
= (6−8/3) − (−7/6)
= 10/3 + 7/6
= 20/6 + 7/6
= 27/6 = 9/2 square units

content.title: Example 3: Area between a curve and the y-axis
content.body:
Find the area between y=x² and the y-axis from y=0 to y=4.

From y=x²: x=√y (taking positive root, right of y-axis).

A = ∫₀⁴ √y dy = ∫₀⁴ y^(1/2) dy
= [(2/3)y^(3/2)]₀⁴
= (2/3)(4)^(3/2) − 0
= (2/3)(8) = 16/3 square units

Alternative using vertical strips:
A = ∫₀² (4−x²) dx = [4x−x³/3]₀² = (8−8/3) = 16/3 ✓

Economic application: In economics, consumer surplus measures the benefit consumers gain from buying at the market price. If the demand curve is p = f(x) (price as a function of quantity) and the market price is p₀, the consumer surplus is the area between the demand curve and the horizontal line p=p₀ from x=0 to the market quantity x₀. This equals ∫₀^(x₀)[f(x)−p₀]dx — a definite integral. The Uganda Revenue Authority uses related calculations to assess the welfare impact of different tax policies.

questions.question: Find the area enclosed by y = x² + 1, the x-axis, x=0 and x=3
questions.explanation: ∫₀³(x²+1)dx=[x³/3+x]₀³=(9+3)−0=12. Since x²+1≥1>0 everywhere, no sign issue.

questions.question: The curve y = x(x−2) dips below the x-axis between x=0 and x=2. Find the ACTUAL area between the curve and x-axis for 0≤x≤2.
Find the area between y = x² and y = 2x from x=0 to x=2
questions.explanation: y=2x is above y=x² on [0,2] (check x=1: 2>1). A=∫₀²(2x−x²)dx=[x²−x³/3]₀²=(4−8/3)=4/3.

questions.question: Find the intersection points of y=x² and y=4, then find the enclosed area.
Find the area bounded by y = sin x and the x-axis from x=0 to x=π
The SIGNED integral ∫₀^(2π) sin x dx gives 0. What does this mean geometrically?
options: There is no area under the sine curve
The area above the x-axis equals the area below, and they cancel
The function is undefined on this interval
questions.answer: The area above the x-axis equals the area below, and they cancel
questions.explanation: On [0,π], sinx>0 (area=2). On [π,2π], sinx<0 (signed area=−2). Total signed integral=2+(−2)=0. The positive and negative areas cancel, but the TOTAL actual area is 4.

questions.question: Find the area enclosed between y=x³ and y=x for x≥0
questions.explanation: Intersections: x³=x → x(x²−1)=0 → x=0,1 (for x≥0). On [0,1]: x≥x³ (check x=0.5: 0.5>0.125). A=∫₀¹(x−x³)dx=[x²/2−x⁴/4]₀¹=1/2−1/4=1/4.

questions.question: For y=x²−4x, find the area between the curve and the x-axis from x=0 to x=4.

lessons.title: Volume of Revolution and Mean Value of a Function

content.body: When a curve is rotated about an axis, it sweeps out a three-dimensional solid of revolution. Integration gives the exact volume of this solid — a powerful tool for computing volumes of bowls, bottles, pipes, domes, and any object with rotational symmetry. Additionally, the definite integral provides the mean (average) value of a function over an interval — useful for finding the average temperature over a day, the average force over a time interval, or the mean value of any continuously varying quantity.
Volume of Revolution About the x-axis

When the region between y=f(x) and the x-axis (from x=a to x=b)
is rotated 360° about the x-axis, the volume of the solid formed is:

V = π ∫ₐᵇ y² dx = π ∫ₐᵇ [f(x)]² dx

Derivation idea: each thin vertical strip (width dx, height y) generates
a thin disc (radius y, thickness dx) when rotated. Volume of disc = πy²dx.
Total volume = sum of all discs = π∫ₐᵇ y²dx.

Note: the y is SQUARED in the formula — this is the disc method.

For rotation about the y-axis:
V = π ∫ₐᵇ x² dy  (swap roles of x and y, integrate w.r.t. y)

content.title: Example 1: Volume of revolution about the x-axis
content.body:
The region bounded by y = x², the x-axis, and x=2 is rotated about the x-axis. Find the volume.

V = π∫₀² y² dx = π∫₀² (x²)² dx = π∫₀² x⁴ dx
= π[x⁵/5]₀²
= π(32/5 − 0)
= 32π/5 cubic units ≈ 20.1 cubic units

content.title: Example 2: Volume of a cone by revolution
content.body:
The line y = rx/h (from origin to point (h,r)) is rotated about the x-axis. This generates a cone of height h and base radius r. Verify the volume formula.

V = π∫₀ʰ y² dx = π∫₀ʰ (rx/h)² dx
= π(r²/h²)∫₀ʰ x² dx
= π(r²/h²)[x³/3]₀ʰ
= π(r²/h²)(h³/3)
= πr²h/3

This is exactly the standard formula V = (1/3)πr²h ✓

The integration confirms the cone formula — a beautiful verification of the disc method.

content.title: Example 3: Volume — region between two curves rotated
content.body:
The region between y=√x and y=x (from x=0 to x=1) is rotated about the x-axis. Find the volume.

Note: on [0,1], √x ≥ x (check x=0.25: √0.25=0.5>0.25 ✓).
The outer radius is √x, the inner radius is x (washer method).

V = π∫₀¹ [(√x)² − x²] dx
= π∫₀¹ [x − x²] dx
= π[x²/2 − x³/3]₀¹
= π(1/2 − 1/3)
= π(3/6 − 2/6)
= π/6 cubic units
Mean Value of a Function

The mean (average) value of f(x) over [a, b]:

 f̄ = (1/(b−a)) × ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx

Geometric interpretation: f̄ is the height of the rectangle with the
same base [a,b] and the same area as ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx.

In other words: area under curve = f̄ × (b−a)

Applications:
• Mean temperature over a time period
• Mean velocity of a particle
• Mean power output of an engine
• Mean value theorem: there exists at least one c in (a,b)
  where f(c) = f̄ (the function actually achieves its mean value)

content.title: Example 4: Mean value of a function
Example 5: RMS (root mean square) value
content.body:
The RMS (root mean square) value of f(x) over [a,b] is:
f_rms = √[(1/(b−a)) ∫ₐᵇ f(x)² dx]

For current I = I₀ sin(ωt) over one period [0, 2π/ω]:
I²_rms = (ω/2π) ∫₀^(2π/ω) I₀² sin²(ωt) dt

Using sin²(ωt) = (1−cos2ωt)/2:
= (ω/2π) × I₀² × (1/2) × [2π/ω]
= I₀²/2

So I_rms = I₀/√2 ≈ 0.707 I₀

This is why Uganda's mains supply of 240V (RMS) has a peak voltage of 240√2 ≈ 339V.

Engineering design: Water storage tanks in Uganda are often designed as solids of revolution — cylindrical or conical. When engineers at the National Water and Sewerage Corporation (NWSC) design a dome-topped cylindrical tank, they calculate the dome's volume using the disc method V=π∫ₐᵇy²dx, where y=f(x) describes the dome's profile curve. Getting the volume right ensures the correct amount of water storage for Uganda's growing urban population.

questions.question: Find the volume when y=3x² (0≤x≤2) is rotated about the x-axis
The region bounded by y=√x, x-axis, x=0, x=4 is rotated about the x-axis. Find the volume.
Find the mean value of f(x)=2x+1 over [0,3]
Find the volume when y=1/x (1≤x≤3) is rotated about the x-axis
Find the mean value of sin x over [0, π]
The region between y=x² and y=x (0≤x≤1) is rotated about the x-axis (washer method). Find the volume.
Using the disc method, verify that rotating y=r (constant) from x=0 to x=h about x-axis gives volume πr²h.
questions.explanation: V=π∫₀ʰy²dx=π∫₀ʰr²dx=πr²[x]₀ʰ=πr²h. This is the volume of a cylinder ✓.

questions.question: Find the RMS value of f(x)=x over [0,2]

key_concepts: Indefinite integral: ∫f(x)dx=F(x)+C where F'(x)=f(x). Always includes +C.
Power rule for integration: ∫xⁿdx=xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1)+C for n≠−1. Special case: ∫x⁻¹dx=ln|x|+C.
Simplify before integrating: expand brackets, split fractions, rewrite surds as powers.
Integration by substitution: let u=g(x), replace g(x)→u and g'(x)dx→du. Reverse chain rule.
sin²x=(1−cos2x)/2 and cos²x=(1+cos2x)/2 — essential for integrating even powers of trig functions.
Fundamental Theorem: ∫ₐᵇf(x)dx=F(b)−F(a). Constant C cancels in definite integrals.
Area under curve: A=∫ₐᵇf(x)dx when f(x)≥0. For regions below x-axis, take absolute value of each section.
Area between curves: A=∫ₐᵇ[f(x)−g(x)]dx where f is upper curve. Find limits from intersection points.
Volume of revolution (x-axis): V=π∫ₐᵇy²dx. Disc method — square y before integrating.
common_errors: Power rule error: adding 1 to the power but forgetting to divide by the new power (or vice versa).
Not simplifying before integrating — trying to integrate a product or fraction directly without expanding or splitting.
In substitution, not changing the limits for definite integrals — must substitute new limits in terms of u.
Taking the integral of a negative region as positive — the integral gives negative values below the x-axis; must take |value| for actual area.
Volume formula error: using V=π∫y dx instead of V=π∫y² dx — must SQUARE y in the disc method.
Forgetting the factor 1/(b−a) in the mean value formula — it is an AVERAGE, not a sum.
